title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Power
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: FortiBleed: ~430,000 Fortinet firewalls swept for 110M+ credentials, now tied to active ransomware — the remote-access doorway into utility OT
items.content_text: Researchers now assess the FortiBleed campaign swept roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls and SSL-VPN gateways — about half of those exposed worldwide across 194 countries — for more than 110 million credentials, and July reporting ties that stolen access directly to active INC Ransom and Lynx ransomware operations. For electric utilities the exposure is direct: these boxes front remote access into EMS/SCADA, substations and the NERC CIP electronic security perimeter, and because credentials leaked rather than a single bug being exploited, no patch closes the hole. An attacker signing in with valid credentials looks like a legitimate engineer, so detection is hard and the window is now. Assume any exposed Fortinet device already sits at the access layer guarding your control network.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: CISA alert 18 Jun; ransomware link reported 2 Jul 2026

items.title: U.S. agencies keep warning that Iran-affiliated actors are logging into internet-exposed PLCs across energy and water
items.content_text: CISA, the FBI, EPA and DOE continue to warn that Iranian-affiliated actors are reaching internet-facing programmable logic controllers across the energy, water and government-facilities sectors, a campaign re-emphasized amid heightened Middle East tensions. For power operators the lesson maps onto distributed generation and auxiliary systems, where solar sites, storage and small substations increasingly expose controllers reachable from the public internet, often shielded only by default or shared credentials. The activity is agency-confirmed rather than a hacktivist boast, and in some cases has produced real operational disruption. The remedy is largely hygiene: get controllers off the public internet, replace default credentials, and hunt for unexpected logic or configuration changes.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: CISA / FBI / EPA / DOE joint alert

items.title: Dragos: KAMACITE spent months mapping U.S. grid control loops for ELECTRUM — and VOLTZITE is inside a U.S. electric utility
items.content_text: Dragos' 2026 electric-grid threat reporting describes KAMACITE, the access team that enables the grid-focused group ELECTRUM, systematically scanning internet-exposed HMIs, variable-frequency drives, meters and cellular gateways across the United States to map entire control loops. Separately, Dragos says VOLTZITE — which receives footholds from the initial-access group SYLVANITE — has been observed operating inside a U.S. electric utility network. The through-line for defenders is that adversaries are investing in patient reconnaissance of the grid edge rather than burning novel exploits, so reducing internet exposure of field devices and watching for reconnaissance is where detection pays off.

items.title: ELECTRUM's attack on Poland's distributed energy resources is the template for grid-edge risk
items.content_text: Dragos attributes a December 2025 attack on operational-technology systems at multiple sites across Poland's electrical grid to ELECTRUM, calling it the first major cyberattack focused on distributed energy resources — wind farms, solar installations and combined-heat-and-power sites. The intrusion targeted the RTUs and communications managing dozens of smaller generation sites, precisely the edge that expands as grids add renewables. For North American operators building out DER, it is a preview: the distributed edge multiplies remotely reachable controllers faster than most utilities can monitor them, and destructive intent is now demonstrated rather than theoretical.

items.title: CISA flags Schneider EasyLogic T150 and Saitel DP RTUs — patch the boxes that talk to your relays
items.content_text: On 30 June CISA published an advisory covering Schneider Electric's EasyLogic T150 and Saitel DP remote terminal units, gear used in energy-sector substations and remote monitoring. RTUs sit at the seam between protection and relaying and the wider control network, so weaknesses here can undermine the integrity of the telemetry and commands operators depend on. Review the advisory for affected firmware, apply the vendor's fixes or mitigations, and keep these devices off any internet-reachable path.

items.title: ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals flagged for the energy sector
items.content_text: Among six ICS advisories CISA released on 2 July was one for ST Engineering iDirect iQ-Series satellite terminals, called out for energy-sector use. Satellite backhaul is how many remote substations and DER sites phone home, so a vulnerable terminal is a foothold at the very edge of the grid where physical oversight is thinnest. Check the advisory for affected models and firmware and apply the vendor's guidance.

items.title: Schneider EcoStruxure IT Data Center Expert advisory names the energy sector
items.content_text: CISA's 30 June batch also covered Schneider Electric EcoStruxure IT Data Center Expert, monitoring software identified for energy-sector deployments. Facility-monitoring platforms straddle IT and OT and often hold broad visibility into power and cooling, making them a useful pivot if left unpatched. Apply the vendor's fixes and confirm the platform is not exposed beyond the management network.

items.title: NERC CIP-012-2 now enforceable (1 July 2026) — tighter protection for control-center data
items.content_text: The revised NERC CIP-012-2 standard became enforceable on 1 July, strengthening requirements to protect the confidentiality and integrity of real-time assessment and monitoring data exchanged between control centers and closing gaps the earlier version left open. Registered entities should confirm their control-center data protection plans, encryption or equivalent controls, and inter-control-center communication paths meet the updated obligations, and refresh their evidence for audit.

items.title: NIST SP 1339 OT Backup Quick Start Guide aims to speed grid recovery
items.content_text: NIST released an OT Backup Quick Start Guide (SP 1339) on 19 June to help industrial operators build recoverable backups and accelerate incident recovery. For utilities, tested and isolated backups of EMS/SCADA, historian and engineering-workstation configurations are what turn a destructive event into a restoration exercise rather than a rebuild — an increasingly concrete concern given demonstrated wiper capability against grid OT. Use it to benchmark your OT backup and recovery program.
